title: Introduction to Parasitic Infections
section: Protozoal and Helminthic Infections General Considerations
authors: Sharon L. Reed, Charles E. Davis

items.title: Understanding Helminth Host Dynamics and Disease Severity
items.content: Helminths use humans as definitive hosts when larvae are ingested, leading to adult development in the intestine with usually mild disease. Conversely, humans serve as intermediate hosts for helminth ova, resulting in larval migration through tissues and potential severe organ invasion.
keyPoints: Humans are definitive hosts for helminths when ingesting larvae; adults develop in the intestine causing mild disease.
As intermediate hosts, ingestion of helminth ova leads to tissue migration and possible severe disease.
Strongyloides can complete its life cycle in humans, unlike most helminths.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.title: Protozoa vs. Helminths: Replication and Disease Impact
items.content: Protozoa can multiply within the human body, potentially causing overwhelming infections by evading immune responses through mechanisms like antigenic variation or intracellular survival.
keyPoints: Protozoa can multiply in humans, unlike helminths which need other hosts for life cycle completion.
Evasion of host immunity is key to protozoan proliferation (e.g., Trypanosoma brucei, Plasmodium).
Naïve patients with first protozoal infections are severely affected due to lack of partial immunity.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.title: Protozoan Risks in Immunocompromised Hosts
items.content: Immunocompromised individuals, particularly those with AIDS, are at risk for disseminated infections from protozoa such as Leishmania, Toxoplasma, Cryptosporidium, and Trypanosoma cruzi.
keyPoints: Disseminated protozoan infections in immunocompromised hosts include Leishmania, Toxoplasma, Cryptosporidium, and Trypanosoma cruzi.
These protozoans are considered AIDS-defining illnesses.
Strongyloides is a helminth that can also disseminate in immunocompromised individuals.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.title: Differential Diagnosis of Intestinal vs. Somatic Tapeworm Infections
items.content: Intestinal tapeworm infections occur when humans ingest larvae from undercooked meat, while somatic infections result from ingesting ova leading to tissue invasion.
keyPoints: Taenia saginata (beef tapeworm), Taenia solium (pork tapeworm), and Diphyllobothrium latum (fish tapeworm) are acquired through ingestion of undercooked meat.
Intestinal infections are usually asymptomatic, except for D. latum causing pernicious anemia in 1–2% of genetically predisposed Scandinavians.
Somatic infections occur when T. solium ova cause cysticercosis or Echinococcus species lead to hydatid cysts.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.title: Clinical Implications of Diphyllobothrium latum Infection
items.content: D. latum can lead to vitamin B₁₂ deficiency and pernicious anemia, particularly in genetically predisposed individuals.
keyPoints: D. latum absorbs vitamin B₁₂ avidly, leading to potential pernicious anemia.
1–2% of infected Scandinavians with a genetic predisposition may develop pernicious anemia.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.title: Pathogenesis and Complications of Schistosomiasis
items.content: Schistosomiasis is caused by blood flukes, with species-specific migration patterns leading to organ-specific complications.
keyPoints: Schistosoma mansoni and S. japonicum migrate to mesentery vessels, causing liver damage and portal hypertension.
S. haematobium targets the urinary tract, leading to repeated infections and kidney damage.
Egg deposition causes granuloma formation and can result in cirrhosis or obstructive uropathy.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.title: Prevalence and Transmission of Intestinal Roundworms
items.content: Intestinal roundworms, including Ascaris lumbricoides, Necator americanus, Ancylostoma duodenale, Trichuris trichiura, Enterobius vermicularis, and Strongyloides stercoralis, are the most common infections globally. They primarily affect resource-poor regions with poor sanitation practices.
keyPoints: Infections caused by intestinal roundworms are the most common worldwide.
Approximately 900 million individuals are infected with Ascaris, hookworms, and Trichuris.
100–370 million people have strongyloidiasis.
Transmission occurs via ingestion of ova or active skin penetration by larvae.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.title: Clinical Manifestations and Risks of Intestinal Roundworms
items.content: Intestinal roundworm infections can lead to serious health issues such as intestinal blockage, malnutrition, anemia, and eosinophilic pneumonia. Strongyloides stercoralis is unique in its ability to reproduce within the body, causing persistent infection.
keyPoints: Heavy Ascaris infections cause intestinal blockage and malnutrition.
Hookworms can lead to anemia due to blood loss.
Strongyloides infection persists over decades and can disseminate when immunocompromised.
Ascaris may cause eosinophilic pneumonia (Loeffler's syndrome) during heavy infections.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.title: Disease Mechanisms of Tissue Roundworms
items.content: Tissue roundworms, such as those causing filariasis, angiostrongyliasis, gnathostomiasis, and trichinellosis, have distinct life cycles involving migration through tissues. Filariasis is the most significant globally, affecting 80 million people.
keyPoints: Filarial worms infect an estimated 80 million individuals in tropical regions.
Lymphatic filariasis, river blindness, and loiasis are caused by different filarial species.
Transmission occurs through bites of infected arthropods.
Recurrent larval migration and adult presence cause lymphatic damage in lymphatic filariasis.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.title: Eosinophilic Meningitis: Differentiating Causes and Severity
items.content: Angiostrongylus cantonensis is the most common parasitic cause of eosinophilic meningitis, while Gnathostoma spinigerum causes a less frequent but more severe form. Both involve larval migration to the brain and meninges, leading to eosinophilic inflammation.
keyPoints: Angiostrongylus cantonensis is most common cause of parasitic eosinophilic meningitis.
Gnathostoma spinigerum causes more severe eosinophilic meningitis with potential for paralysis or brain hemorrhage.
Most individuals recover spontaneously from A. cantonensis infection.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 2) | INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.title: Diagnosis and Severity of Entamoeba histolytica Infection
items.content: Entamoeba histolytica causes invasive disease with dysentery or bloody diarrhea, which can disseminate to the liver. It must be differentiated from noninvasive E. dispar, as they appear identical under microscopy.
keyPoints: E. histolytica causes dysentery or bloody diarrhea.
Differentiation from bacterial infections like Salmonella is crucial.
Cannot diagnose by cyst/trophozoite identification due to similarity with E. dispar.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 2) | INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.title: Naegleria Fowleri: Rapidly Fatal Meningitis from Warm Water Exposure
items.content: Naegleria fowleri, a free-living amoeba found in warm freshwater environments like lakes and hot springs, causes acute pyogenic meningitis. This condition is almost universally fatal despite treatment with miltefosine, which shows in vitro activity against the organism.
keyPoints: Acquired in warm lakes or hot springs
Causes sudden pyogenic and usually fatal meningitis
Miltefosine active in vitro but CNS infection remains nearly always fatal
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.title: Plasmodium falciparum: High Mortality Malaria with Severe Complications
items.content: Plasmodium falciparum, prevalent in sub-Saharan Africa, is responsible for over 80% of malaria infections and 90% of malarial deaths. It invades any erythrocyte, leading to high parasite loads and potential resistance to antimalarial drugs.
keyPoints: Most common in sub-Saharan Africa
Causes more than 80% of malaria infections and 90% of malarial deaths
Invades any erythrocyte, reaches very high parasite loads
Damages organs by adhering to vascular epithelium
High likelihood of drug resistance
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.title: Toxoplasma gondii: Diverse Clinical Manifestations Based on Host Factors
items.content: Toxoplasma gondii is a ubiquitous obligate intracellular parasite. Its clinical manifestations vary with the host's age and immune status, ranging from asymptomatic to severe congenital infections or fatal reactivation in immunocompromised individuals.
keyPoints: Infection follows ingestion of oocysts or tissue cysts
Clinical manifestations depend on host's age and immune status
Congenital infection can cause severe outcomes early in pregnancy
Reactivation during immunosuppression (AIDS, organ transplantation) can be fatal
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.title: Importance of Travel History in Diagnosing Parasitic Infections
items.content: A detailed travel history is crucial for diagnosing parasitic infections, especially since many serious ones are uncommon in the U.S. The duration and nature of travel to endemic areas significantly influence risk.
keyPoints: Travel history is critical for diagnosing parasitic infections.
Greater risk with longer stays in endemic areas.
Increased risk factors include immunocompromise, adventure itineraries, rural travel, war zones, or refugee camps.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Parasitic Infection

items.title: Timing of Symptom Onset and Its Diagnostic Implications
items.content: The timing of symptom onset relative to travel is key in narrowing differential diagnoses for parasitic infections. Fever starting >10–14 days after leaving an endemic area often rules out certain diseases.
keyPoints: Malaria should be considered first in febrile patients returning from endemic areas.
Fever beginning >10-14 days post-travel typically excludes dengue, chikungunya, and Zika.
Persistent fever months after return suggests malaria.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Parasitic Infection

items.question: Which of the following organisms is NOT typically classified as a parasite under the conventional definition used for helminths and protozoa?
items.explanation: The designation 'parasite' is reserved by convention for helminths and protozoa, not bacteria or viruses.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.question: What is the primary reason that finding effective antiparasitic agents has historically been challenging?
options: Parasites are smaller than human cells.
Parasites have a prokaryotic cell structure.
Parasites have a eukaryotic cell structure similar to human host cells.
Parasites do not cause disease in humans.
items.explanation: The similarity of the eukaryotic cell structure of parasites with that of human host cells has made it difficult to find effective antiparasitic agents without causing toxicity to human cells.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.question: In which scenario do humans serve as definitive hosts for helminths?
options: When they ingest helminth ova that develop into larvae.
When they ingest helminth larvae that develop into adults in the intestine.
When they act as intermediate hosts allowing larval development.
When they are exposed to environmental sources of adult helminths.
items.explanation: Humans serve as definitive hosts when they ingest helminth larvae, which develop into adults in the intestine.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.question: Which of the following protozoa is known for evading the host immune response by antigenic variation?
items.explanation: Trypanosoma brucei evades the host immune response through antigenic variation.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.question: Which tapeworm is commonly referred to as the beef tapeworm?
items.explanation: Humans acquire Taenia saginata by eating insufficiently cooked beef.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.question: What condition can Diphyllobothrium latum cause in a small percentage of infected individuals with a genetic predisposition?
items.explanation: D. latum avidly absorbs vitamin B₁₂ and can cause pernicious anemia.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.question: Which species of Echinococcus is primarily transmitted in sub-Arctic areas?
items.explanation: E. multilocularis is transmitted primarily in sub-Arctic areas.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.question: Which organ do Schistosoma mansoni and S. japonicum larvae migrate to after penetrating the skin?
items.explanation: S. mansoni and S. japonicum migrate to the mesentery vessels and eventually make their way to the liver.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.question: Which intestinal roundworm is most commonly associated with eosinophilic pneumonia, also known as Loeffler's syndrome?
items.explanation: Ascaris lumbricoides occasionally causes eosinophilic pneumonia (Loeffler's syndrome) during heavy infections.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.question: Which intestinal roundworm is most prevalent in the United States and other developed countries?
items.explanation: Pinworms (Enterobius vermicularis) are the most common causes of intestinal roundworm infection persisting in developed countries.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.question: Which tissue roundworm is responsible for causing river blindness?
items.explanation: River blindness is caused by Onchocerca volvulus.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.question: Which intestinal roundworm can reproduce within the human body, potentially leading to disseminated infection when the immune system is compromised?
items.explanation: Strongyloides infection can persist over decades and disseminate when the immune system is compromised.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.question: Which of the following roundworm infections is most commonly associated with eosinophilic meningitis?
items.explanation: Angiostrongylus cantonensis is the most common parasitic cause of eosinophilic meningitis.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 2) | INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.question: Which roundworm infection has been almost eradicated?
items.explanation: Guinea worm infection (dracunculiasis) has been almost eradicated.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 2) | INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.question: Which roundworm larvae show a preference for migrating to the skin, eyes, and meninges?
items.explanation: Gnathostoma spinigerum larvae show a preference for the skin, eyes, and meninges.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 2) | INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.question: Which free-living amoeba is associated with causing Acanthamoeba keratitis after trauma to the eye and exposure to contaminated water?
items.explanation: Acanthamoeba causes Acanthamoeba keratitis after trauma to the eye and exposure to contaminated water.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.question: Which species of Plasmodium is most common in sub-Saharan Africa and causes more than 80% of malaria infections?
items.explanation: Plasmodium falciparum is most common in sub-Saharan Africa and causes more than 80% of malaria infections.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.question: Which protozoan parasite is transmitted by ixodid ticks and can cause severe infection in asplenic patients?
items.explanation: Babesia is transmitted by ixodid ticks and can cause severe infection in asplenic patients.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.question: Which species of Trypanosoma causes Chagas disease, primarily found in South and Central America?
items.explanation: Trypanosoma cruzi causes Chagas disease, which is primarily found in South and Central America.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.question: Which factor does NOT increase the risk of acquiring serious parasitic infections for travelers?
items.explanation: Even with adherence to appropriate malarial chemoprophylaxis, the risk of malaria is not eliminated.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Parasitic Infection

items.question: When should malaria be considered as a diagnosis in a febrile patient returning from an endemic area?
options: If fever began within 10–14 days after departure
If symptoms started before the travel
Only if the patient was not immunized
If fever begins several months or later after return
items.explanation: Malaria must be considered first in a febrile patient returning from an endemic area.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Parasitic Infection

items.question: Which of the following is most likely to cause persistent travelers' diarrhea?
items.explanation: Travelers' diarrhea that persists for weeks is more likely to be parasitic in origin.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Parasitic Infection

items.statement: Protozoa, like bacteria, can multiply within the human body and cause overwhelming infections.
items.explanation: Protozoa have the ability to multiply within the human body, similar to bacteria.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.statement: Most helminths can complete their life cycle and multiply entirely within humans without requiring other hosts.
items.explanation: Almost all helminths require stages in other hosts to complete their life cycles and multiply.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.statement: Intestinal fluke infections are usually symptomatic.
items.explanation: Infection with intestinal flukes is usually asymptomatic.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.statement: Echinococcus granulosus can cause hydatid cysts.
items.explanation: Both E. granulosus and E. multilocularis cause hydatid cysts.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.statement: Intestinal roundworm infections are most common in developed countries.
items.explanation: Infections caused by intestinal roundworms are most common in resource-poor developing countries.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.statement: Lymphatic filariasis can develop into significant disease during short-term travel (<3 months) to an endemic region.
items.explanation: It is unusual for the short-term traveler to develop significant lymphatic filariasis.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.statement: Eosinophilic meningitis caused by Gnathostoma spinigerum is often more severe than that caused by Angiostrongylus cantonensis.
items.explanation: Gnathostoma spinigerum can cause more severe eosinophilic meningitis, potentially leading to paralysis or brain hemorrhage.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 2) | INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.statement: Cryptosporidium is resistant to high levels of chlorine and can cause severe diarrhea in immunocompromised patients.
items.explanation: Cryptosporidium is highly infectious, chlorine-resistant, and can cause severe diarrhea in immunosuppressed individuals.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 2) | INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.statement: Naegleria infection acquired from warm lakes or hot springs causes sudden pyogenic and usually fatal meningitis.
items.explanation: Naeglerial meningitis, acquired in warm lakes or hot springs, is indeed sudden, pyogenic, and usually fatal.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.statement: Plasmodium vivax can invade any erythrocyte, similar to Plasmodium falciparum.
items.explanation: Plasmodium vivax invades only reticulocytes with Duffy antigen, unlike P. falciparum which can invade any erythrocyte.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.statement: Most travelers who acquire malaria have taken appropriate prophylaxis.
items.explanation: Most travelers who acquire malaria have taken inadequate or no prophylaxis.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Parasitic Infection

items.statement: Typhoid fever is a common cause of prolonged fever in immunized individuals.
items.explanation: Typhoid fever is much less likely to be the cause of prolonged fever in an immunized individual.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Parasitic Infection

items.assertion: Naïve patients with their first protozoal infection are often the most severely affected.
items.reason: Partial immunity limits the number of parasites during recurrent infections.
items.explanation: The reason correctly explains why naïve patients experience more severe effects from their first protozoal infection.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.assertion: Immunocompromised individuals are at risk of disseminated helminthic infections.
items.reason: Strongyloides can disseminate in immunocompromised hosts.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, it is not universally applicable to all helminths; only Strongyloides is noted for dissemination in immunocompromised individuals.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Introduction to Parasitic Infections

items.assertion: Ingesting Taenia solium ova can lead to somatic infection.
items.reason: The larvae penetrate the intestine and migrate into tissue, forming cysts.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.assertion: Fasciola hepatica can only be acquired in sheep-raising areas.
items.reason: It is especially common where animals ingest water plants like watercress.
items.explanation: Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · HELMINTHIC INFECTIONS | FLATWORMS

items.reason: Heavy hookworm infections lead to intestinal blockage and malnutrition.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the assertion.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.assertion: Loiasis often results in visible symptoms such as adult worms migrating across subconjunctival tissue.
items.reason: Many infected individuals with loiasis are asymptomatic and only diagnosed when a worm is visible.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 1)

items.assertion: Entamoeba histolytica causes invasive disease characterized by dysentery or bloody diarrhea.
items.reason: The diagnosis of amebiasis cannot be made solely by identifying cysts or trophozoites, as they are identical to those of noninvasive E. dispar.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; however, the reason does not explain why Entamoeba histolytica causes invasive disease.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · ROUNDWORMS (part 2) | INTESTINAL PROTOZOA

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.assertion: Acanthamoeba causes brain masses and skin lesions in immunocompromised individuals.
items.reason: Invasive infection with Acanthamoeba occurs due to its ability to cause severe disease in those with weakened immune systems.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why Acanthamoeba causes brain masses and skin lesions in immunocompromised individuals.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.subtopic: FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS
items.assertion: Plasmodium vivax is the dominant cause of malaria outside sub-Saharan Africa.
items.reason: Plasmodium vivax reaches higher levels of parasitemia than Plasmodium falciparum.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false; P. vivax reaches lower levels of parasitemia compared to P. falciparum.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · FREE-LIVING AMOEBAS | BLOOD AND TISSUE PROTOZOANS

items.assertion: A thorough history and physical examination are essential for diagnosing parasitic infections.
items.reason: Many serious parasitic infections are uncommon in the United States, making travel history critical.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason supports the importance of a thorough history.
items.reference: Introduction to Parasitic Infections · Parasitic Infection
